transcript.transcript: We started, I guess it would have been in 95 or 96, the same year LeMass was going into Christmas in 95. We started, I did, started taking them to lunch every year at the end of the rose season, after they had finished deadheading for one of the last times. And we would take them to lunch, and we would buy them each a box of chocolate. So I would go to those and thank them for all their hard work. And I think they were very appreciative of that. And they always brought it up every year, how much bad times they gave me. And I think most of them felt kind of bad. And now, it's 10 years later, and there pretty much is beginning to be a new crop of folks that are coming in to some of the people that had been doing it and passed away. So it's a tradition I think a lot of people have done that really like it. And of course, they have their social in the park that raises funds. And then from those monies, they go out and buy award-winning rose bushes. And those are put into the park. And they're significant in the fact that they're all different varieties, different colors, different smells, different sizes. And they are all award-winning roses. And we've had, since I've been here, an employee that we would call Hook, because he wasn't married anymore. And on the back of his truck, it said something like, fishing's better than being married, something like that. And so they took the name Hook, I guess, from the fact that he liked to fish a lot. And he just took great care of the rotospar. And he also took great care of the folks that were going there to do all the deadheading. So we did call them deadhead. You did? I was going to ask. Yeah, we did. And they actually really liked it. But now he is gone. He retired this year. So we're training someone new. I think filling those shoes is going to be a little bit tough. They don't have any qualms about saying which roses aren't looking good and why aren't you spraying them to take care of the aphids. You're not pruning them back correctly. I think they're pretty... They know their stuff. Yeah, they are. They're pretty finicky. Now, did you do the update of the gazebo there Under your administration? Let's see. Yeah, we did, actually. That might have been 2001, 2002 project. The old gazebo had a vine that was growing through the gazebo. And it was basically just tearing it apart. And it was what was holding the old gazebo up. It was massive. It was four or five inch trunks coming up and then branches. It grew really fast. And I know because I have one in my backyard. My little gazebo and it's starting to tear apart. I've got to take it out before mine gets ripped. So yeah, we took it out. And we have some money. We received some donations and we replaced it. It's actually very attractive. There were some worries that it would change the character, but it's actually enhanced it. And there's weddings that go on there during Any time of the day, probably during the summer, people pass through and watch the wedding and continue to look at the roses. I know because I've been there. And we're done at the weddings. Doesn't seem to cause any problems for the wedding. More people showed up to the wedding than anticipated. You don't have receptions there, though, do you? No, they can rent the shelters and have receptions. And there's a shelter space. I don't know how often that occurs. I'm sure pretty much every new group has the opportunity to use it as long as they want to save the reservation. The other thing is you have to really pretty much book that activity well in advance. Because if you didn't, you're going to have to change usually again the date or the location. First thought after one comes to the use. One thing that you and I have worked on that involves Julia Davis Park, but not something that people really think of as Julia Davis Park is a cross Capitol Boulevard to the west. Is that not still park land, or Tom Davis' gift for the log cabin and the antique memorial art? That was part of the original gift, yeah. And people miss that, I think, that that was part of the park. I think it was part of the library was originally part of the park. It definitely was the log cabin, literary arts center, and the Anne Frank Human Rights Memorial. And that's the Anne Frank Memorial took place through the administration. Yep, Sure did. Wonderful event. That was a lot of fun. I probably look back at that one as one of the most rewarding and fortunate ones that I've ever had to work on. I was the one that took the group out, wanted to look at a place to site such a memorial and I took them out and said, I think this is really the place I wanted to do it is along the river. It has the intersection of 8th Street and the alley going back into the park. It's kind of at a crossroads with the Green Velvet. And they ran with it. It's been probably so well accepted that it has a great partnership between the city and the group that did all the fundraising. Uh-huh. Uh-huh. Good. Some of Those are fun ones that you like to remember. Right. Right. But I don't think most people think of, when they think of Julia Davis Park, they don't think of that land where the log cabin is or where the Anne Frank Memorial is. But it's actually a part of the park, I guess. We didn't. I think, obviously, the park was there in 1907. And I don't think Capitol Boulevard came into play until later. I'm not sure what the timing was on that. So yeah, it was dissected by Capitol Boulevard. And then some of the property was added. It's 83 or 84 acres now. I can't remember the acres of every park. But it was, I think The original first gift was about 56 acres. And then some gifting was by the MK company, which had done some of the dredging for the one pond is now. So once they were finished, that property went to the city. I really would have to go back and look at the history on that when that occurred. It seemed to me it was in the, sometimes, the 50s. Might have even been earlier than that. I've talked to some people who talk about going ice skating a lot in the pond. Has that occurred at all during your administration? Not really. Too much global warming? Yeah, I think that's a significant factor. And it's one that we, if you do it, it's at your own risk. We're not out here checking the ice. I wouldn't do it. I mean, To me, it's kind of dangerous, even though the water's not that deep. When you get out into the pond, it's also had a tremendous number of years where Ducks and geese have been there, and so the bottom is not sanitary. And that's one of the things we are looking at doing in the long run, is to get in there and physically dress that out. The challenge is, where do you take it, and What do you do with it? So that'll be an interesting project. An interesting pond. An interesting project. Yeah, it's going to be. You've got to do it in the wintertime. And that's going to be difficult. We know it's going to be tough because we've gone in and as you look at the shoreline around the pond and the channel going down through the zoo, we've put in what's called gabion baskets. There really wasn't much there. There was some rock before, some rock wall. It was staling, and the pond was sliding off the shoreline. So we went in and put in gabing baskets, which are wire baskets with large rocks in them. And we contoured around the lake, the pond, or the lake, whichever one you want to call it. And it actually improved the quality of what you could see. We could have some more expensive treatment, put that into concrete like you see in the Boston Commons Park downtown. Boston, it's cost prohibitive. And I think over time, vegetation will come up through a lot of that. It already has started doing that. And you're going to open up a creek in the park? Yeah, actually two. The first one that will go in will be the discharge from the Boise City, where the water flows into Julia Davis Park now, the pond. So it'll be discharged on the southern side of the road, coming out of the zoo. And that'll be opened up and flow into the river. We're working with Trout Unlimited, who has a grant. They will open it up, place rocks and gravels in the bottom, and shrubs, river-type plantings to create what was really there in the first place. And then the other one is the Boise City Canal, I think it's Boise City, that actually goes, no it's not 4CC, it's one that comes out of Cottonwood Creek. It's an old, basically a drain and goes from Cottonwood Creek down through basically military, Fort Boise community park, goes down through a neighborhood immediately adjacent to East Junior High. It goes that towards, from there it's basically underground. From there, underneath Broadway, and then underground all the way to the river. So we're going to go to that intersection between Myrtle and Broadway, and we'll open it up and take it all the way to the river. And it'll look pretty much the same. It'll be contoured. It'll have gravels and rock and that type of planting. And get it back to really what it used to be a very long time ago. Great. Do you think of anything else you want to tell us? No, I'm sure I will. It's another fun time. I covered everything that I know. I appreciate it very, very much. 15 years. Outstanding art director. I was very fortunate to have you. Oh, thank you. It's flattering. Very, very nice. Thank you so much. You bet. This has been Jim Hall at Boise, Idaho, November 13, 2006.

lines.line: # Beginning Traditions

> The speaker discusses the tradition of taking employees to lunch after the rose season and providing them with a box of chocolates as a token of appreciation. This tradition has become cherished and is followed by newer generations as well.
** tradition, lunch, appreciation, rose season, chocolates **

** Julia Davis Park, log cabin, Anne Frank Memorial, park land **

** park expansion, land gift, pond safety, maintenance **

> Plans to open up creeks and improve environmental conditions in the park are underway, in collaboration with organizations like Trout Unlimited. These projects aim to restore natural waterways.
